schools.summary: Henry reads the queen as a pattern for all who seek Christ (Matt. 12:42): those who honour God he honours, and true wisdom is worth any pains, the pearl of great price.
Ellicott traces the chronicler's abridgment of Kings — the omitted 'name of Jehovah,' the 'hard questions' as riddles (cf. Judg. 14:12), the favourite phrase 'gold in abundance.'
Clarke outlines the chapter and refers the visit to 1 Kings 10, noting the Targum's 'queen of Zemargad.'
Henry reads Solomon's answering of every question as the freeness of a God-given gift: freely he received, freely he gave (Matt. 10:8) — wisdom ministered, not hoarded.
Keil and Delitzsch reproduce the queen's overwhelmed testimony, letting the heaped detail of wisdom, house, table, and ascent testify to the greatness of the gift.
Henry reads the catalogue as the beauty of good order — the propriety of court and worship, the king's ascent to God's house (cf. Ps. 42:4) — as what overwhelmed the queen.
Ellicott judges 'his ascent' a likely transcription error for the 'burnt offering' of Kings (so LXX, Syr., Vulg.), the Hebrew word elsewhere meaning 'upper chamber.'

debates.question: Is 'his ascent by which he went up' the true reading, or a slip for 'his burnt offering'?

sides.position: a transcription error for the 'burnt offering' of Kings, supported by the versions
the received text, the king's stately ascent to the house of the LORD

themes: the ascent to the house of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'thine acts' as literally 'thy words' (so LXX), embracing Solomon's sayings and deeds alike; Keil and Delitzsch carry the queen's mounting testimony.
Henry hears the vindication of the wisdom-seeker: she staked a costly journey on a report and found it true, well repaid for her pains.
Ellicott notes the chronicler's explanatory addition 'the greatness of thy wisdom' and the literal 'thou hast added to the report,' narrowing the queen's wonder to Solomon's wisdom.
Henry reads 'the half was not told' as the highest praise — the reality outran the rumour, as the wisdom of God always exceeds its fame.

Henry reads the beatitude as the blessing of constant converse with the wise — Solomon's servants so honoured that their descendants were content to be 'children of Solomon's servants' (Ezra 2:55; Neh. 7:57).
Ellicott notes the chronicler's added conjunctions ('and happy... and hear') weaken the abrupt rhetorical force the beatitude has in Kings.
Ellicott presses 'his throne' as Jehovah's throne (cf. 1 Chron. 28:5), Solomon the LORD's vicegerent; the added clauses are the chronicler's settled theology, not tampering.
Wesley: 'for the LORD' means in God's name and stead, Solomon sitting in God's own throne over God's peculiar people.
Henry: we should bless God for others' gifts; the queen traces king and kingdom to the divine favour — 'because thy God delighted in thee' and 'loved Israel.'

Ellicott glosses 'spices' (balsam/balm) and 'great abundance,' and reads 'neither was there any such spice' as 'there had not been such spicery in Jerusalem,' a deviation from Kings owing to a defective manuscript.
Clarke reckons the 120 talents of gold at nearly 900,000 pounds — a kingdom's tribute laid before Solomon as homage.
Henry reads the exchange of gifts as the generosity of the wise: valuing wisdom above money, queen and king confirm friendship by mutual presents.

themes: the queen's gift of gold and spices

schools.summary: Ellicott recasts the Ophir trade as the Tyrian-Israelite partnership (cf. 2 Chron. 8:18) and canvasses the identity of the 'algum' — red sandalwood, coral-hued wood, or (Thenius) Indian teak.
Henry reads the curious imports as prosperity's pull toward things rare but useless, warning that wealth and true enjoyment keep no such alliance as wisdom and happiness do.

Ellicott weighs 'terraces' (raised paths/stairways, interpreting a rare word of Kings; LXX 'ascents,' Vulg. 'steps') and hears in 'the land of Judah' the post-exilic writer for whom the fallen north has passed from view.
Clarke and Wesley take 'beside that which she had brought' of Solomon's royal bounty far exceeding her gifts (cf. 1 Kings 10:13) — not a returning of her own presents.
Ellicott weighs equal return-presents or a parenthetic note that giving was not all on one side, and reports Bertheau's emendation 'what the king had brought for her'; the versions differ.
Henry reads the exchange as generosity meeting generosity, the seal of a friendship contracted in the love of wisdom.

debates.question: What is meant by 'beside that which she had brought unto the king'?

sides.position: return-gifts of Solomon's bounty far exceeding hers (as Kings makes plain)
a slight emendation to 'what the king had brought for her'

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the verse coincides word for word with 1 Kings 10:14.
Clarke reckons the 666 talents of yearly gold at nearly five million pounds, a revenue no contemporary throne could match.

Henry reads the golden revenue as the promised riches of 2 Chron. 1:12 fulfilled, a type of Messiah's glory (yet outshone by the lilies, Matt. 6:29), and hears mercy in the chronicler's silence about Solomon's fall (Ezek. 33:16).

Ellicott finds the 'chapmen and merchants' phrase corrupt (Thenius: 'tributes of the subjects,' backed by Syr./LXX), reads 'kings of Arabia' for 'kings of the mixed tribes,' and 'brought' as habitual tribute; Keil carries the text into the golden catalogue.
Clarke adds the Targum's 'kings of Sistevantha' for the kings of Arabia.
Henry reads the neighbours' gifts as freewill homage, a type of the wise men bringing gold to the infant Christ (Matt. 2:11).

debates.question: What lies behind the corrupt phrase 'that which chapmen and merchants brought'?

sides.position: 'men of the travellers,' i.e. traders' traffic

themes: type of the wise men's gold

schools.summary: Ellicott (verse identical with 1 Kings 10:16) reads 'beaten gold' as gold-leaf and 'went to' as 'he plated' the large oblong targets (cf. Amos 8:10).
Clarke reckons each target a fifth of a talent, the 200 together some 300,000 pounds — gold shields for show, not war.
Henry reads the golden targets as Solomon's public splendour, dazzling to the eye yet only a faint type of the Messiah's kingdom, whose throne is above every throne.

Ellicott distinguishes the smaller round shield from the great target (cf. Ps. 35:2) and weighs the discrepancy of 'three hundred shekels' against the older 'three manehs' — a transcription error or a wrongly supplied 'shekels' for 'drachms.'
Henry reads the golden shields and targets as Solomon's parade of glory, stored in the cedar-pillared house of the forest of Lebanon, yet only the artificial lustre of an earthly throne.

debates.question: How much gold went to each shield — 'three hundred shekels' or 'three manehs'?

sides.position: 'three hundred shekels' is a transcription slip, or 'shekels' should read 'drachms'
'three minas,' as the older text and the Syriac/Arabic have it

themes: the house of the forest of Lebanon

schools.summary: Ellicott notes 'pure' is the common word replacing a rare term of Kings — the chronicler modernizing as he copies — the throne ivory sheathed in gold.
Clarke reproduces at the chapter's end a Persian legend of the throne (a genie's work, golden lions, singing mechanical birds), relayed as a specimen of eastern exaggeration, not history.
Henry reads the unequalled throne as a type of the Messiah's kingdom, a faint representation of the throne that is above every throne.

outliers.note: Reproduces a legendary Persian account of Solomon's throne — genie-built, with golden lions and singing mechanical birds — as a curiosity of eastern lore rather than commentary on the text.

themes: the ivory throne overlaid with gold

schools.summary: Ellicott suspects the 'footstool of gold' a corruption of the older 'rounded top behind' (Syriac agrees; LXX omits the footstool), the Hebrew here awkward; the rest as 1 Kings 10:19.
Henry reads the six-stepped, lion-flanked throne as Solomon's unequalled state and a type of the Messiah's kingdom, the lions the emblem of Judah and David's house.

themes: the steps and lions of the throne

schools.summary: Ellicott defends the supplied 'none were of silver' (agreeing with 1 Kings 10:21): silver so common in Solomon's reign that it counted for nothing, the royal plate all of gold.
Henry reads silver 'nothing accounted of' as the vanity of wealth — wisdom and happiness are united, but not wealth and true enjoyment; only the Saviour gives rest.

Ellicott weighs whether 'ships went to Tarshish' is an erroneous paraphrase of the older 'Tarshish fleet' (ships for long voyages, Isa. 2:16) or a real Mediterranean fleet trading westward, distinct from the Red Sea venture.
Clarke records the Targum's 'went to Africa,' a sign of ancient uncertainty over Tarshish's location.
Henry reads the triennial voyages as a wise prince's enterprise, yet the apes and peacocks among curious things prosperity taught Solomon to love.

debates.question: Does 'the king's ships went to Tarshish' misread the older 'Tarshish fleet'?

sides.position: an erroneous paraphrase, turning a class of ship into a destination
holders: the general assumption (as reported by Ellicott)

sides.position: possibly a real westward Mediterranean fleet, distinct from the Ophir venture

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'passed all' as 'was great above all' (cf. 1 Kings 10:23) — the chronicler's crowning estimate, riches and wisdom named together.
Henry reads Solomon's supremacy as the promise fulfilled and a type of Christ, in whom are hid all treasures of wisdom, though the princes of this world neglect him (1 Cor. 2:8).

themes: type of a greater than Solomon

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'all the kings of the earth' as defining the vaguer 'all the earth' of Kings; Keil gathers the closing verses into one summary estimate drawn from the older records.
Wesley trims 'all the kings of the earth' to all in those parts of the world within reach of Solomon's fame.

Henry: the wisdom was God's gift 'put in his heart'; the kings' seeking it shames the world's contempt of Christ, in whom are hid all treasures of wisdom, yet whom the princes will not seek (1 Cor. 2:14).

themes: wisdom God put in his heart
a rebuke to those who neglect Christ

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'they brought' as habitual, 'harness' as weapons and armour, and 'a rate year by year' as the fixed annual due of vassal kings; Keil sets it in the summary of Solomon's greatness.
Henry reads the yearly tribute as the homage of the nations and a type of Christ, to whom the wise men brought gifts (Matt. 2:11) and to whom believers bring themselves (Rom. 12:1).

themes: type of the wise men's gifts

schools.summary: Ellicott takes 'four thousand stalls' as correct against the 'forty thousand' of 1 Kings 4:26, and notes the chronicler avoids repeating the fuller account of 2 Chron. 1:14; Keil traces the verse to an earlier source, not the Kings horse-trade passage.
Clarke refers to the discrepant numbers at 1 Kings 4:26 and records the Targum's 'four hundred.'

debates.question: How many stalls for horses did Solomon have?

sides.position: four thousand (our text), the true reading
forty thousand, as 1 Kings 4:26 has it (Ellicott: an error)

schools.summary: Ellicott ties the verse to 1 Kings 4:21 and reads it as defining 'the earth' by its bounds — Euphrates to Philistia to Egypt; Keil sets it among the reign's drawn-together notices, citing the parallel under the Hebrew numbering (1 Kings 5:1).

themes: dominion from the river to Egypt

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the verse is identical with 1 Kings 10:27 and sets beside it the royal prohibitions of Deut. 17:16ff., so the boasted abundance stands under the law's warning against multiplying silver and gold.
Henry contrasts earthly and heavenly treasure: abundance cheapens gold but the increase of grace raises its price — better to get wisdom than gold.

Ellicott reads 'they brought' as habitual and the verse as summarizing 1 Kings 10:28, adding 'out of all lands'; Keil notes the whole horse-trade is here rounded off into one general statement — the mention of Egypt echoing the prohibition Ellicott flagged at the previous verse.

themes: horses out of Egypt and all lands
the shadow of the forbidden trade

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'the rest of the acts' as 'the words'/history, ties Ahijah to 1 Kings 11:29, and notes 'Solomon' was originally identical with Shalman, an Assyrian divine name borne by a king of Moab.
Clarke: the books of Nathan, Ahijah, and Iddo are all lost; Wesley: these prophets were also historians who wrote the annals from which the sacred books were drawn.
Henry hears vanity and mercy — Solomon leaving all to a fool (Eccl. 2:19), and the Holy Ghost's silence over the king's fall as a sign of sin repented, pardoned, and remembered no more.

outliers.note: Observes that the name 'Solomon,' though 'peace' to the Hebrew ear, was originally identical with Shalman, an Assyrian divine name, borne by a Salamanu king of Moab under Tiglath-pileser.

schools.summary: Ellicott ties the verse to 1 Kings 11:42 and weighs 'over all Israel' as the undivided nation, soon to be rent under Rehoboam; Keil reads it as the plain notice of the reign's length.
Henry recalls the forty years as a reign of profound peace, unlike the war-famed princes — the favour of God under a king whose name meant peace.

Ellicott reads 'slept' as literally 'lay down' and notes our 'he was buried' for the older 'they buried him,' the texts otherwise identical; Keil takes it as the plain notice of death, burial, and succession.
Wesley: Solomon in his throne and in his grave — the throne could not secure him from the grave; he leaves all to one he knew would be a fool, vanity and vexation of spirit.

themes: the throne no shield from the grave
vanity and the division to come
